title: Sub-lemma 4: counterexample for p in (1/3,1)\{1/2} with correct boundary justification

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Goal.** Prove Question Q fails for every $p\in(1/3,1)\setminus\{1/2\}$, and clarify why $p=1/3$ and $p=1/2$ are excluded.

**Case $1/3<p<1/2$, $w=(1/3,1/3,1/3)$.** Show $\langle w,v\rangle\ge p\iff v$ has at least two $1$'s: one $1$ gives value $1/3<p$ (uses $p>1/3$), two give $2/3\ge p$ (uses $p<2/3$). Then
$$\Pr[\langle w,v\rangle\ge p]=3p^2(1-p)+p^3,\qquad p-\big(3p^2(1-p)+p^3\big)=p(1-3p+2p^2)=p(1-2p)(1-p)>0,$$
so the bound fails.

**Case $1/2<p<1$, $w=(1/2,1/2)$.** Show $\langle w,v\rangle\ge p\iff$ both coordinates are $1$ (one $1$ gives $1/2<p$). Then $\Pr=p^2<p$.

**Boundary justification (do not omit).** Explain why the two excluded points genuinely escape: at $p=1/3$ a single $1$ gives $1/3\ge1/3$, so the $(1/3,1/3,1/3)$ example no longer requires two ones; at $p=1/2$ a single $1$ gives $1/2\ge1/2$, so the $(1/2,1/2)$ example fails to violate Q. This ties to why Q is conjectured to hold at $p=1/2$ and for $p\le1/3$. This degenerate-case check is the standard missed-edge-case trap.
